# SwarmForge Constitution

This file takes precedence over article files.
Read and obey every file in `swarmforge/constitution/articles/`.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/roles/specifier.prompt">
You are the specifier.

## Owns
- Own externally visible behavior specifications, acceptance criteria, examples, and end-to-end QA suite specifications.
- Ask questions to settle ambiguity.
- Turn user intent into precise, testable behavior without prescribing unnecessary implementation details.
- On receiving a handoff, run `git reset --hard origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')` to start from the current default branch.
- On startup, check `.agents/backlog.md` if it exists; surface any `pending` entries as candidate tasks to the user before writing a new spec.

## Specification Rules
- Keep specifications concise and deterministic.
- Separate feature files by behavior and technology.
- Name each scenario with the feature name and a stable index, and include that scenario name in a comment immediately preceding each feature.
- Use the Gherkin format defined by github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification.
- Gherkin will be mutation tested; use Gherkin parameters for any fields that might vary.
- Start every feature from `swarmforge/templates/feature.feature`; complete all eight header sections before writing scenarios. `none` is valid for empty sections.

## End-To-End QA Suite
- Also produce an end-to-end QA suite for each feature.
- End-to-end means the QA suite operates at the user interface and does not use an API into the project.
- Command-line flags and special QA commands are allowed when they are user-interface affordances exposed to the QA agent.
- The QA suite should specify user-visible workflows, inputs, outputs, and observable states that QA can verify independently of implementation internals.
- If the feature has UX requirements: search project root and common locations (docs/, design/, assets/) for DESIGN.md, EXPERIENCE.md, and mockup/wireframe files; record found paths under `Design artifacts` in the UX INTENT section. Consult them when writing the four observable dimensions (Visual Composition, Information Hierarchy, Interaction Feel, State Transitions). If none are found, write `none`.

## Feature Workflow
- For each feature, work in six phases:
  1. Write the Gherkin that specifies the feature.
  2. Prune the Gherkin so parameters are only values germane to Gherkin acceptance testing; remove redundant parameters that do not improve Gherkin acceptance mutation.
  3. Use `ir-dry-checker` to normalize and prune the Gherkin.
  4. Move repeated scenario setup into a Gherkin `Background` when doing so preserves scenario meaning.
  5. Write the end-to-end QA suite that verifies the feature through the user interface without using a project API; include command-line flags or special QA commands only when they are user-interface affordances.
  6. Ask the user for approval to hand off to the coder.

## Verification
- Do not run Gherkin acceptance mutation.
- Run tests when verification is needed; do not run other verification or quality tools.

## Handoff
- Do not commit or notify coder until the user explicitly approves the handoff. After approval, commit the specification changes, invent a short stable task name, and notify coder using the file-based handoff format with that name in the `task:` header.
- When the curator notifies you that the job is complete, ask the user for the next feature to add.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/project.prompt">
# Project Rules

## Project Shape
- This project is configured for SwarmForge with nine Codex-backed agents: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, and curator.
- Project language: Babashka.

## Local Configuration
- Preserve project-local SwarmForge configuration under `swarmforge/`.
- Keep swarm state local under `.swarmforge/`, worktrees under `.worktrees/`, and shared scripts under `swarmforge/scripts/`.

## Handoffs
- Prefer terse, explicit handoffs that report state and request role-appropriate review. Do not include verifications or sender process narrative.

## Ownership

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>

<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.

attachment.content:
- agent-retro: Run a conversation retrospective — analyze what happened in this session, what worked, what didn't, and propose concrete improvements. Use when the user says "retro", "retrospective", "what happened in this session", "session review", "what did we do", "analyze this conversation", or when wrapping up a long session. Especially useful after using a skill you're developing. In swarmforge: invoked automatically as the last step before each role goes idle.
- explain: Explains the intent behind source code by finding original session transcripts. Use explain with a function, file, or line of code to understand why it exists.
- recall: Use when the user describes a task and wants to know whether something similar has been done before, then turn the closest prior session into a task playbook. Triggers on phrases like "have we done this before", "recall how we did X", "find similar work", "any precedent for", "has anyone solved", "is there a template for", and "how did we do this last time"
- replay: Use when the user wants to step through a feature's checkpoints chronologically, pausing at each step to ask questions. Triggers on phrases like "replay <feature>", "walk me through how X was built", "show me the journey of", "step me through how Y was implemented", and "replay the last week"
- review: Review code changes on the current branch using checkpoint transcript context to understand developer intent before auditing the diff. Use when the user asks for a code review, wants to review branch changes, or asks to audit recent work before merging.
- search: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
- session-crosslink: Use when an agent session ran outside the repo whose commits should record it — e.g. launched from a higher-level folder, a non-Entire repo, or one repo but editing another — to attach the session to each affected Entire-enabled repo's HEAD commit.
- session-handoff: Use when the user wants to continue work from one agent in another agent, inspect recent sessions, or summarize a saved session or checkpoint for handoff
- session-to-skill: Use when the user wants to turn one or more Entire-tracked sessions, checkpoints, or repeated agent workflows into a reusable agent skill.
- setup-swarm: One-time project setup for SwarmForge. Run before the first `./swarm` launch. Installs language-appropriate quality tools, wires session tracking, writes permission allow-rules, and scaffolds .gitignore. Triggers on "setup swarm", "setup the swarm", "/setup-swarm", "first time setup", or "prepare project for swarm".
- swarm-persona: Load this agent's SwarmForge role, constitution, and operating instructions
- teach: Use when a developer wants a topic-focused guided lesson built from canonical checkpoints, not a whole-repo overview. Triggers on phrases like "teach me <topic>", "teach me how this repo handles", "how does <topic> work in this repo", "give me a lesson on", "school me on", and "I need to learn about"
- using-entire: Use for any codebase exploration or understanding task — reads historical intent from Entire checkpoints instead of guessing. Orchestrates other Entire skills to give the agent provenance-backed answers about code.
- what-happened: Explain why code looks the way it does by tracing the latest change for a file range or pasted snippet through `git blame` and deduplicated `entire explain` lookups. Use when the user asks what happened, says "tell me why" about a code block, is confused about a section of code, asks "wtf is going on", "why is this like this", "why was this changed", or wants provenance for a specific file block.
- plannotator-annotate: Open interactive annotation UI for a markdown file
- plannotator-last: Annotate the last rendered assistant message
- plannotator-review: Open interactive code review for current changes or a PR URL
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-querying-skill: Scaffolds a new privilege-separated `query-<name>` CLI plus its matching `querying-<name>` skill, using a sudo-gated outer/inner script split so an LLM agent can invoke the tool without reading the credentials. Use when the user wants to wrap a new API, database, or remote system as a query tool, or asks to "create a new querying skill".
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-release-plan: Authors, edits, reviews, and maintains Addi release plans as operational manuals. Use when user says 'create release plan', 'edit release plan', 'review release plan', or 'add release plan rule'.
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-tech-spec: Authors, edits, and reviews Addi tech specs against the canonical TS9999 structure. Use when the user wants to "create a tech spec", "edit a tech spec", "review a tech spec", or "propose changes to a tech spec".
- gabadi-plugin:managing-cianbox: Queries and manages the Cianbox ERP API — productos, marcas, categorias, listas_precio, ajuste_stock, clientes, pedidos, estados_pedidos, ventas, ml_ventas, cotizaciones, webhook. Reads and writes both supported. Use when inspecting Cianbox data or performing authorized writes.
- gabadi-plugin:managing-kommo: Queries and manages the Kommo CRM API — leads, contacts, notes, pipelines, tasks, events, users, custom fields. Reads (GET) by default; writes (POST/PATCH/DELETE) gated per-env by kommo_mode=rw. Use when inspecting Kommo data or performing authorized writes against a Kommo account.
- gabadi-plugin:managing-metabase: Queries and manages the deployed Metabase instance — databases, tables, collections, saved questions (cards), dashboards, and ad-hoc SQL. Read (GET) by default; admin writes (POST/PUT/DELETE) gated per-env by metabase_mode=rw. sql and run commands always allowed. Use when inspecting Metabase data, running queries, or performing authorized admin operations.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-addi-endpoints: Query Addi platform HTTP endpoints against sandbox, staging, or local port-forwards — verify a new implementation works, debug a live 500, or check how a client is evaluated end-to-end. Use when you need real cluster data and services, not mocks. Not for production.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-alegra: Queries the Alegra accounting API. Use when inspecting ERP data — bank accounts, payments, invoices, contacts, categories. Access mode (ro/rw) is per-account in the secret file.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-configcat: Manages ConfigCat feature flags — list, inspect targeting rules, enable/disable flags per attribute (tenant, user, role). Use when toggling features for specific tenants or inspecting flag state.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-databases: Runs read-only SQL against Addi database environments (PostgreSQL and MySQL). Use when inspecting DB state or validating data. SELECT/WITH only.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-grafana: Queries Grafana Loki logs and Prometheus metrics. Use when investigating production/staging issues or searching logs. Note: auth token expires, may need refresh.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-remote-logs: Queries Docker container logs on remote machines via SSH. Use when investigating issues on remote hosts, viewing container output, or listing running containers.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

You are the specifier.

## Owns
- Own externally visible behavior specifications, acceptance criteria, examples, and end-to-end QA suite specifications.
- Ask questions to settle ambiguity.
- Turn user intent into precise, testable behavior without prescribing unnecessary implementation details.
- On receiving a handoff, run `git reset --hard origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')` to start from the current default branch.
- On startup, check `.agents/backlog.md` if it exists; surface any `pending` entries as candidate tasks to the user before writing a new spec.

## Specification Rules
- Keep specifications concise and deterministic.
- Separate feature files by behavior and technology.
- Name each scenario with the feature name and a stable index, and include that scenario name in a comment immediately preceding each feature.
- Use the Gherkin format defined by github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification.
- Gherkin will be mutation tested; use Gherkin parameters for any fields that might vary.
- Start every feature from `swarmforge/templates/feature.feature`; complete all eight header sections before writing scenarios. `none` is valid for empty sections.

## End-To-End QA Suite
- Also produce an end-to-end QA suite for each feature.
- End-to-end means the QA suite operates at the user interface and does not use an API into the project.
- Command-line flags and special QA commands are allowed when they are user-interface affordances exposed to the QA agent.
- The QA suite should specify user-visible workflows, inputs, outputs, and observable states that QA can verify independently of implementation internals.
- If the feature has UX requirements: search project root and common locations (docs/, design/, assets/) for DESIGN.md, EXPERIENCE.md, and mockup/wireframe files; record found paths under `Design artifacts` in the UX INTENT section. Consult them when writing the four observable dimensions (Visual Composition, Information Hierarchy, Interaction Feel, State Transitions). If none are found, write `none`.

## Feature Workflow
- For each feature, work in six phases:
  1. Write the Gherkin that specifies the feature.
  2. Prune the Gherkin so parameters are only values germane to Gherkin acceptance testing; remove redundant parameters that do not improve Gherkin acceptance mutation.
  3. Use `ir-dry-checker` to normalize and prune the Gherkin.
  4. Move repeated scenario setup into a Gherkin `Background` when doing so preserves scenario meaning.
  5. Write the end-to-end QA suite that verifies the feature through the user interface without using a project API; include command-line flags or special QA commands only when they are user-interface affordances.
  6. Ask the user for approval to hand off to the coder.

## Verification
- Do not run Gherkin acceptance mutation.
- Run tests when verification is needed; do not run other verification or quality tools.

## Handoff
- Do not commit or notify coder until the user explicitly approves the handoff. After approval, commit the specification changes, invent a short stable task name, and notify coder using the file-based handoff format with that name in the `task:` header.
- When the curator notifies you that the job is complete, ask the user for the next feature to add.

# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

## Parent

#1 — PRD: drywall — polyglot AST subtree DRY analyzer

## What to build

Add JavaScript and TypeScript as analysis targets using tree-sitter-javascript and tree-sitter-typescript grammars. The same normalization and Jaccard pipeline from the Rust target applies unchanged; only the `LangConfig` (identifier/literal node type names) and function form detection are new.

```gherkin
Given two TypeScript files each containing an arrow function with identical structure but different variable names
When I run drywall on the directory
Then both are reported as a duplicate pair

Given two JavaScript files each containing a function declaration with identical structure but different variable names
When I run drywall on the directory
Then both are reported as a duplicate pair

Given a .ts file in the analyzed directory
When I run drywall without --lang
Then it is analyzed as TypeScript automatically

Given a .js file in the analyzed directory
When I run drywall without --lang
Then it is analyzed as JavaScript automatically

Given I run drywall with --lang ts on a file with a non-standard extension
Then it is analyzed as TypeScript

Given a directory containing both .rs and .ts files
When I run drywall on the directory
Then each file is analyzed with its own grammar
And results from both languages appear in a single output
```

### JS/TS function forms to recognize

Function declarations, arrow functions assigned to `const`/`let`, class methods, exported named functions. JSX/TSX files included (`.jsx`, `.tsx`).

### Extension auto-detection

`.js`, `.jsx` → JavaScript grammar; `.ts`, `.tsx` → TypeScript grammar.

### LangConfig

Identifier node type names and literal node type names for JS/TS grammars must be mapped so the shared normalization pipeline produces `_ID` and `_LIT` tokens correctly.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Two TS fixture files with structurally identical arrow functions (different variable names) → pair reported
- [ ] Two JS fixture files with structurally identical function declarations → pair reported
- [ ] `.ts` file auto-detected as TypeScript without `--lang`
- [ ] `.js` file auto-detected as JavaScript without `--lang`
- [ ] `--lang ts` forces TypeScript grammar on any file
- [ ] Mixed directory with `.rs` and `.ts` files → both analyzed, results interleaved in output
- [ ] Dogfood still passes: `drywall ./src` exits 0

## Blocked by

#4

title:	PRD: drywall — polyglot AST subtree DRY analyzer (Rust/JS/TS/Python)
state:	OPEN
author:	gabadi
labels:	ready-for-agent
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
issue-type:	
parent:	
sub-issues:	
sub-issues-completed:	
blocked-by:	
blocking:	
number:	1
--
## Problem Statement

Engineering teams working across Rust, JavaScript/TypeScript, and Python codebases have no tool that detects **semantic duplication** — functions that implement the same logic with different variable names. Existing tools (jscpd, cargo-dupes) detect copy-paste through token-sequence matching but miss independent reimplementations of the same algorithm. Uncle Bob's DRY tools (`dry4go`, `dry4clj`, `dry4java`) implement the correct approach (AST subtree hashing + Jaccard similarity) but only exist for Go, Clojure, and Java. There is no equivalent for Rust, JS/TS, or Python.

The result: duplicated business logic silently accumulates across services and stacks. Two teams independently implementing the same scoring function, validation rule, or data transformation have no automated signal that consolidation is possible.

## Solution

**drywall** is a single static Rust binary implementing Uncle Bob's DRY algorithm across Rust, JavaScript/TypeScript, and Python source targets. It uses tree-sitter grammars uniformly for all three languages, applies the same AST subtree normalization + Jaccard similarity pipeline regardless of source language, and produces output format-compatible with `dry4go` for drop-in adoption. It dogfoods itself — the Rust target is the first implemented and validates the algorithm on drywall's own source before shipping.

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to run `drywall ./src` on a Rust project and see a ranked list of function pairs that are structurally duplicated, so that I can identify consolidation opportunities.
2. As a developer, I want to run `drywall ./src` on a JS/TS project and get the same output format as on a Rust project, so that I don't need to learn different tools per stack.
3. As a developer, I want to run `drywall ./src` on a Python project and get the same output format, so that polyglot monorepos are covered by a single tool invocation.
4. As a developer, I want drywall to detect that two functions are duplicates even when all variable names differ, so that independently written implementations of the same logic are surfaced.
5. As a developer, I want drywall to NOT flag two short trivial functions as duplicates, so that noise from boilerplate is suppressed.
6. As a developer, I want to configure the Jaccard similarity threshold via `--threshold`, so that I can tune sensitivity for my codebase's idiom frequency.
7. As a developer, I want to configure the minimum function size gates via `--min-lines` and `--min-nodes`, so that I can suppress noise from trivially small functions.
8. As a developer, I want to force a specific language via `--lang`, so that I can analyze files with non-standard extensions or mixed-language directories.
9. As a developer, I want to exclude paths via `--exclude`, so that generated code, vendor directories, and test fixtures don't pollute results.
10. As a developer, I want machine-readable output via `--format json`, so that I can pipe results into CI dashboards or other tooling.
11. As a developer, I want the default text output to show the file paths, line ranges, and similarity score for each duplicate pair, so that I can navigate directly to the relevant code.
12. As a CI engineer, I want drywall to be a single static binary with no runtime dependencies, so that I can drop it into any CI environment without managing Node.js, Python, or Rust toolchains.
13. As a CI engineer, I want drywall to exit with a non-zero code when duplicates above the threshold are found, so that I can use it as a merge gate.
14. As a developer, I want drywall to recurse directories and auto-detect language from file extension, so that I don't need to enumerate files manually.
15. As a developer, I want drywall to skip `.git`, `target`, `node_modules`, `__pycache__`, and `vendor` directories automatically, so that I don't need to exclude them every run.
16. As a developer, I want drywall to analyze its own Rust source and report no duplicates above threshold (dogfood gate), so that the correctness of the algorithm is validated on a real codebase.
17. As a developer, I want the output to be sorted by similarity score descending, so that the worst violations appear first.
18. As a developer, I want each reported pair to include the node count for both functions alongside the score, so that I can judge whether the match is substantive or shallow.
19. As a Rust developer, I want drywall to understand Rust function definitions, impl blocks, closures, and trait implementations as separate analysis units, so that all code units are covered.
20. As a JS/TS developer, I want drywall to understand function declarations, arrow functions, class methods, and exported functions as separate analysis units, so that the full range of JS/TS function forms is covered.
21. As a Python developer, I want drywall to understand `def` functions, class methods, and async functions as separate analysis units, so that all Python function forms are covered.
22. As a developer, I want identifier normalization to treat all local variable names as equivalent, so that renaming a variable doesn't break a duplicate match.
23. As a developer, I want literal normalization to treat all string, numeric, and boolean literals as equivalent within structural context, so that changing a magic number doesn't break a duplicate match.
24. As a developer, I want drywall to be fast enough to run on a monorepo with thousands of files in under 10 seconds, so that it's practical as a pre-commit or CI check.

## Implementation Decisions

### Uniform tree-sitter architecture

All three target languages are parsed using tree-sitter grammars (`tree-sitter-rust`, `tree-sitter-javascript` + `tree-sitter-typescript`, `tree-sitter-python`). All parsers operate on tree-sitter's generic `Node` type, enabling a single shared traversal and normalization pipeline. Language-specific configuration is limited to a `LangConfig` struct per language declaring which node type name strings are identifiers, which are literals, and which are structural. Adding a new language target requires ~20-30 LOC (grammar dependency + one `LangConfig` entry).

This was chosen over OXC (JS/TS) + syn (Rust) to keep the architecture uniform. OXC's richer semantic model is not needed for subtree hashing — identifier/literal distinction is sufficient, and tree-sitter provides that for all three languages.

### Algorithm

For each qualifying function in each source file:
1. Parse to tree-sitter AST
2. Walk every node, collecting all subtrees at every nesting depth
3. Normalize each subtree: identifier nodes → `_ID` token, literal nodes → `_LIT` token; operators, keywords, and structural tokens preserved as-is
4. Serialize normalized subtree to a canonical string and hash (FNV-1a or xxHash)
5. Record `(file, fn_name, start_line, end_line, HashSet<u64>)` per function

After collecting all functions:
1. Build inverted index: `hash → Vec<FunctionRef>`
2. Candidate pairs: any two functions sharing ≥ 1 hash
3. For each candidate pair: Jaccard = `|A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|`
4. Report pairs where Jaccard ≥ threshold AND both functions pass qualification gates

### Qualification gates

Default: ≥ 4 source lines AND ≥ 20 normalized AST nodes. Both gates must pass. Functions below these thresholds are skipped entirely. Rationale: below this size trivial functions match each other universally, collapsing signal-to-noise.

### CLI interface

```
drywall [options] [path ...]
```

| Flag | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `--threshold` | `0.82` | Minimum Jaccard similarity to report |
| `--min-lines` | `4` | Minimum source lines to qualify |
| `--min-nodes` | `20` | Minimum normalized AST nodes to qualify |
| `--lang` | auto-detect | `rs`, `js`, `ts`, `py` — force language |
| `--format` | `text` | `text` or `json` |
| `--exclude` | — | Glob patterns to exclude (repeatable) |

Auto-excluded always: `.git/`, `target/`, `node_modules/`, `__pycache__/`, `vendor/`, `dist/`, `.next/`

### Text output format (mirrors dry4go)

```
DUPLICATE score=0.89
  src/billing/invoice.rs:12-25
  src/billing/receipt.rs:30-44  (88 nodes / 91 nodes)
```

### JSON output format (mirrors dry4go)

```json
{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "score": 0.89,
      "left":  {"file": "src/billing/invoice.rs", "start_line": 12, "end_line": 25, "nodes": 88},
      "right": {"file": "src/billing/receipt.rs", "start_line": 30, "end_line": 44, "nodes": 91}
    }
  ]
}
```

### Exit codes

- `0` — no duplicates above threshold found
- `1` — one or more duplicate pairs reported
- `2` — error (parse failure, invalid arguments, unreadable path)

### Implementation order (language targets)

1. **Rust** (`tree-sitter-rust`) — first target; validates algorithm by running drywall on its own source (dogfood). Rust function forms: `fn`, `impl` methods, closures assigned to `let` bindings, trait implementations.
2. **JS/TS** (`tree-sitter-javascript` + `tree-sitter-typescript`) — second target. Function forms: function declarations, arrow functions, class methods, exported named functions.
3. **Python** (`tree-sitter-python`) — third target. Function forms: `def`, class `def` methods, `async def`.

### Distribution

Single static binary via `cargo build --release`. No runtime dependencies. Cross-compilation to Linux/macOS/Windows via `cross` or `cargo-zigbuild`. WASM via `wasm-pack` is deferred.

### Scaling

Validated on the largest known target repo: ~350 qualifying fragments → ~61,000 candidate pairs → ~6M hash comparisons. Runs in under 1 second in Python equivalent; Rust will be substantially faster. Brute-force O(n²) Jaccard is sufficient — no MinHash or LSH needed.

## Testing Decisions

A good test for drywall tests observable CLI behavior (stdout, exit code) given fixture input files. It does NOT test internal data structures (hash sets, inverted index, intermediate representations). Fixtures are minimal synthetic source files — small enough to reason about by hand, large enough to pass the qualification gates.

### What to test

- **Algorithm correctness**: two fixture functions structurally identical modulo identifier/literal names → score = 1.0; two completely different functions → score below threshold; pair not reported when one function is below gate
- **Qualification gates**: functions below `--min-lines` or `--min-nodes` excluded; CLI flag changes take effect
- **Threshold flag**: pair with score 0.75 reported at `--threshold 0.70`, not at `--threshold 0.80`
- **Language auto-detection**: `.rs` → Rust grammar; `.ts` → TypeScript grammar; `.py` → Python grammar
- **`--lang` override**: file with non-standard extension analyzed correctly when language forced
- **JSON output**: `--format json` produces valid JSON with correct shape
- **Exit codes**: `0` when no duplicates, `1` when duplicates found, `2` on bad input
- **Auto-exclusions**: files inside `node_modules/` or `target/` ignored even when path is explicitly passed
- **Dogfood test**: `drywall ./src` on drywall's own Rust source exits `0`

### Testing seams

- **Primary**: CLI integration tests — spawn the compiled binary as a subprocess, pass fixture directories, assert on stdout and exit code. This is the highest viable seam.
- **Secondary**: Unit tests only for the normalization function (tree-sitter node → normalized string), as this is the most algorithm-sensitive component and the only case where a unit test is more diagnostic than a CLI test.

## Out of Scope

- WASM / npm distribution (deferred)
- Sub-function block-level duplicate detection (only whole-function Jaccard; block extraction is a future extension)
- Cross-language duplicate detection (a Rust function matching a Python function is not in scope)
- IDE integration or LSP server
- Historical trending or per-PR delta reporting
- Configuration file (CLI flags only)
- Languages beyond Rust, JS/TS, Python
- Threshold auto-calibration
- crap4js patches (tracked separately)
- Python CRAP script (tracked separately)

## Further Notes

**Reference implementation:** `dry4go` (github.com/unclebob/dry4go) is the behavioral reference. drywall's text and JSON output formats mirror it exactly.

**Threshold calibration:** dry4go's `0.82` threshold was tuned for Go. JS and Python have different idiom distributions. The default may need calibration after first real-world runs on those stacks — this is a known open question, not a blocker.

**Relation to jscpd v5:** jscpd detects copy-paste (token-sequence); drywall detects semantic duplication (same structure, different names). They are complementary. Running both on a JS/TS codebase surfaces both classes of violation.

**Dogfood commitment:** drywall's CI must run `drywall ./src` as a build step. Any self-duplicate above threshold is a build failure. This is both a correctness gate and a design constraint.

**Full background research:** `docs/tool-analysis-crap-dry-mutation.md` in `gabadi/gabadi-swarm-forge` contains the complete analysis covering all three tool families (CRAP, DRY, mutation), all stacks (JS/TS, Python, Rust), full LCOV format spec, and open questions.

## Problem Statement

Engineering teams working across Rust, JavaScript/TypeScript, and Python codebases have no tool that detects **semantic duplication** — functions that implement the same logic with different variable names. Existing tools (jscpd, cargo-dupes) detect copy-paste through token-sequence matching but miss independent reimplementations of the same algorithm. There is no equivalent for Rust, JS/TS, or Python.

4	Feature file: `features/duplicate_detection.feature`
5	
6	## Scope
7	
8	Verifies the duplicate-detection contract entirely through the command-line
9	interface. QA spawns the compiled binary as a subprocess, passes fixture
10	directories and flags, and observes only exit code, stdout, and stderr. QA does
11	not call any project API and does not inspect internal structures (parser, AST,
12	hash sets, inverted index).
13	
14	The user-interface affordances exercised are the positional `<path>` arguments
15	and the flags `--threshold`, `--min-lines`, `--min-nodes`, `--format`, `--lang`,
16	and `--exclude`.
17	
18	## Preconditions
19	
20	- The release binary is built (`cargo build --release`) at
21	  `target/release/drywall`.
22	- Fixtures are created on disk under a QA-controlled temp directory (e.g.
23	  `./tmp/qa-dd/<case>/`); QA writes `.rs` files, never touches project internals.
24	
25	## Fixture builders (QA-side, on disk only)
26	
27	- **STRUCTURAL-TWIN**: two `.rs` files whose functions have identical control
28	  flow and statement structure but different identifier and literal text; each
29	  function is large enough to clear the default gates (≥ 4 lines, ≥ 20 nodes).
30	- **UNRELATED**: two `.rs` files with functions of different structure (e.g. an
31	  accumulating loop vs. a config-parsing match), each clearing the gates.
32	- **TINY-LINES**: a `.rs` file whose function is 3 source lines (below the
33	  4-line default) but otherwise a structural twin of a second file's function.
34	- **TINY-NODES**: a `.rs` file whose function has ~15 normalized nodes (below the
35	  20-node default) but otherwise a structural twin of a second file's function.
36	- **TWO-PAIRS**: a fixture set producing two distinct duplicate pairs with clearly
37	  different scores (e.g. ~0.95 and ~0.84) so ordering is observable.
38	- **UNPARSABLE**: a `.rs` file containing syntactically invalid Rust.
39	
40	## User-visible workflows
41	
42	### QA-1 — Structural twin reported as a duplicate pair (exit 1)
43	- Setup: STRUCTURAL-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-dd/twin/`.
44	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/twin`
45	- Expected exit code: `1`
46	- Expected stdout: one `DUPLICATE` block naming both fixture files with their
47	  line ranges; the `score=` value is ≥ `0.82`.
48	- Expected stderr: empty.
49	
50	### QA-2 — Unrelated functions produce no pair (exit 0)
51	- Setup: UNRELATED in `./tmp/qa-dd/unrelated/`.
52	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/unrelated`
53	- Expected exit code: `0`
54	- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line (empty, or a "no duplicates" line if the
55	  implementation prints one — QA asserts the absence of any pair).
56	- Expected stderr: empty.
57	
58	### QA-3 — Dogfood: drywall on its own source exits clean (exit 0)
59	- Input: `drywall ./src` run from the project root.
60	- Expected exit code: `0`
61	- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line.
62	- Expected stderr: empty.
63	- Rationale: the dogfood gate — drywall's own source must contain no pair that
64	  scores at or above the default threshold under the default gates.
65	
66	### QA-4 — Function below the line gate is not a candidate (exit 0)
67	- Setup: TINY-LINES in `./tmp/qa-dd/tiny-lines/`.
68	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/tiny-lines`
69	- Expected exit code: `0`
70	- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line.
71	- Rationale: a function with fewer than 4 source lines is not a candidate, so no
72	  pair forms even though a structural twin exists.
73	
74	### QA-5 — Function below the node gate is not a candidate (exit 0)
75	- Setup: TINY-NODES in `./tmp/qa-dd/tiny-nodes/`.
76	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/tiny-nodes`
77	- Expected exit code: `0`
78	- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line.
79

81	- Setup: TWO-PAIRS in `./tmp/qa-dd/two-pairs/`.
82	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/two-pairs`
83	- Expected exit code: `1`
84	- Expected stdout: two `DUPLICATE` blocks; the block with the higher `score=`
85	  appears before the block with the lower `score=`.
86	- Determinism: running the same input twice yields byte-identical stdout.
87	
88	### QA-7 — `--threshold` raises the reporting cutoff
89	- Setup: STRUCTURAL-TWIN scoring ~0.84 in `./tmp/qa-dd/thresh/`.
90	- Input A: `drywall --threshold 0.90 ./tmp/qa-dd/thresh`
91	  - Expected exit code: `0`; stdout has no `DUPLICATE` line.
92	- Input B: `drywall --threshold 0.80 ./tmp/qa-dd/thresh`
93	  - Expected exit code: `1`; stdout has one `DUPLICATE` block.
94	
95	### QA-8 — `--min-lines` override qualifies a short function
96	- Setup: TINY-LINES (3-line twin) in `./tmp/qa-dd/min-lines/`.
97	- Input: `drywall --min-lines 2 ./tmp/qa-dd/min-lines`
98	- Expected exit code: `1`; stdout reports the pair that the default gate
99	  suppressed.
100	
101	### QA-9 — `--min-nodes` override qualifies a small function
102	- Setup: TINY-NODES (~15-node twin) in `./tmp/qa-dd/min-nodes/`.
103	- Input: `drywall --min-nodes 10 ./tmp/qa-dd/min-nodes`
104	- Expected exit code: `1`; stdout reports the pair that the default gate
105	  suppressed.
106	
107	### QA-10 — `--format json` with duplicates emits parseable JSON (exit 1)
108	- Setup: STRUCTURAL-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-dd/json/`.
109	- Input: `drywall --format json ./tmp/qa-dd/json`
110	- Expected exit code: `1`
111	- Expected stdout: valid JSON containing exactly one pair object with `score`,
112	  and `left`/`right` objects each carrying `file`, `start_line`, `end_line`,
113	  and `nodes`.
114	- Expected stderr: empty.
115	
116	### QA-11 — `--format json` with no duplicates emits empty array (exit 0)
117	- Setup: UNRELATED in `./tmp/qa-dd/json-empty/`.
118	- Input: `drywall --format json ./tmp/qa-dd/json-empty`
119	- Expected exit code: `0`
120	- Expected stdout: valid JSON equal to `[]`.
121	
122	### QA-12 — `--exclude` suppresses a matching path
123	- Setup: STRUCTURAL-TWIN where the right file lives under
124	  `./tmp/qa-dd/excl/vendor/`.
125	- Input: `drywall --exclude "**/vendor/**" ./tmp/qa-dd/excl`
126	- Expected exit code: `0`; stdout has no `DUPLICATE` line, because one member of
127	  the only candidate pair was excluded from scanning.
128	
129	### QA-13 — `--lang rust` forces analysis of a non-standard extension
130	- Setup: STRUCTURAL-TWIN whose files use a non-`.rs` extension (e.g. `.rsx`) in
131	  `./tmp/qa-dd/lang/`.
132	- Input: `drywall --lang rust ./tmp/qa-dd/lang`
133	- Expected exit code: `1`; stdout reports the pair, confirming the forced
134	  language overrode extension-based detection.
135	
136	### QA-14 — Nonexistent path is an error (exit 2)
137	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/does-not-exist`
138	- Expected exit code: `2`
139	- Expected stderr: non-empty diagnostic naming the unreadable path.
140	- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line.
141	
142	### QA-15 — Unparsable source is an error (exit 2)
143	- Setup: UNPARSABLE in `./tmp/qa-dd/unparsable/`.
144	- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/unparsable`
145	- Expected exit code: `2`
146	- Expected stderr: non-empty diagnostic.
147	- Rationale: distinguishes a parse failure (exit 2) from a clean no-duplicate
148	  run (exit 0).
149	
150	## Observable states summary
151	
152	| State | How QA observes it |
153	|---|---|
154	| Duplicates found | exit code `1` AND ≥ 1 `DUPLICATE` block (or ≥ 1 JSON pair object) |
155	| No duplicates | exit code `0` AND no `DUPLICATE` block (JSON: `[]`) |
156	| Error | exit code `2` AND non-empty stderr diagnostic |
157	| Deterministic output | identical input run twice yields byte-identical stdout |
158	| Sorted output | higher `score=` block precedes lower `score=` block |
159	
160	## Out of scope for this suite
161	
162	- JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python analysis (Rust target only this slice).
163	- `--lang` values other than `rust`.
164	- Cross-pair clustering or transitive grouping.
165	- Internal verification of normalization, hashing, or Jaccard math (covered by
166	  the project's unit tests, not by this CLI-only QA suite).

- Setup: UNPARSABLE in `./tmp/qa-dd/unparsable/`.
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-dd/unparsable`
- Expected exit code: `2`
- Expected stderr: non-empty diagnostic.
- Rationale: distinguishes a parse failure (exit 2) from a clean no-duplicate
  run (exit 0).

## Observable states summary

| State | How QA observes it |
|---|---|
| Duplicates found | exit code `1` AND ≥ 1 `DUPLICATE` block (or ≥ 1 JSON pair object) |
| No duplicates | exit code `0` AND no `DUPLICATE` block (JSON: `[]`) |
| Error | exit code `2` AND non-empty stderr diagnostic |
| Deterministic output | identical input run twice yields byte-identical stdout |
| Sorted output | higher `score=` block precedes lower `score=` block |

## Out of scope for this suite

- JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python analysis (Rust target only this slice).
- `--lang` values other than `rust`.
- Cross-pair clustering or transitive grouping.
- Internal verification of normalization, hashing, or Jaccard math (covered by
  the project's unit tests, not by this CLI-only QA suite).

Verifies the scaffold's user-visible contract through the command-line interface

content.text:
Good answers. Two settled; one needs a quick response on cross-language complexity.

**On cross-language pair complexity:** It's *not* a big implementation lift mechanically — the pipeline already produces a single global inverted index of normalized subtree hashes, so a `.rs` function and a `.ts` function that happen to normalize to overlapping hashes would *already* become candidate pairs unless explicitly partitioned by language. So "allow cross-language" is arguably *less* code (no partitioning needed).

**But** the PRD explicitly lists it as out of scope, and there's a real correctness problem: Rust and JS/TS grammars use *different node-type names* and produce *different tree shapes* for analogous constructs (e.g. a Rust `if` vs a JS `if_statement`, different operator token spellings). Normalized hashes across grammars are not semantically comparable — a cross-grammar hash collision is structural noise, not a real duplicate. So allowing cross-language pairs would produce **false positives that are hard to reason about**, and it contradicts the PRD's stated scope.

My recommendation: **keep pairs within the same language for this slice** (the safe, PRD-aligned choice), and spec it as an explicit `Does NOT` in scope. The "complexity" of *adding* it later is low; the complexity of *trusting* its output is high. That's the real reason it's deferred.

questions.question: Given that cross-language pairing is mechanically cheap but produces semantically-untrustworthy matches (different grammars → non-comparable normalized hashes) and the PRD lists it out of scope — how should I spec it for #5?

options.description: Pairs form only within one language; mixed dirs interleave each language's own pairs in one sorted output. Spec'd as explicit Does NOT. PRD-aligned, no false-positive risk. (Recommended)

options.label: Allow cross-language
options.description: Permit .rs×.ts pairs. Cheaper code but contradicts PRD scope and risks noisy cross-grammar false positives. Would expand PRD scope.

content.text:
Understood — you've chosen to **allow cross-language pairs**, expanding PRD scope for this slice. I'll spec it that way. To be precise: a normalized Rust function and a normalized JS/TS function whose subtree-hash sets overlap enough to clear the threshold *will* be reported as a duplicate pair.

Decisions locked in:
1. **--lang**: accept `js`, `ts`, keep `rust` (backward compatible).
2. **Cross-language pairs**: **allowed** — pairs may span languages; mixed-dir output is one sorted list including any cross-language pairs.
3. **JSX/TSX**: all `.jsx` and `.tsx` route through the **TypeScript** grammar.

Plus from the issue: `.js`→JS grammar, `.ts`/`.tsx`/`.jsx`→TS grammar, `--lang` forces grammar on any extension, JS/TS function forms = function declarations, arrow functions bound to const/let, class methods, exported named functions.

The DRY checker found one finding: the `left_file`/`right_file` Given steps are structurally identical (the same step with `left_`/`right_` placeholder variants). This is expected and intentional — it's the canonical "left file" + "right file" setup pair used throughout the existing `duplicate_detection.feature` (which has the same shape). It's a `placeholder-variant`, not a true duplication; the two steps describe two distinct files.

This is the same pattern the prior feature uses and is exactly what **Phase 4 (Background extraction)** can address where it preserves meaning. But here the two steps differ in their parameters per-row (`left_ids` vs `right_ids` differ within a row), so they cannot collapse into a Background without losing the per-file distinction. This is an acceptable, semantically-required variant — consistent with how `duplicate_detection.feature` was accepted.

**Phase 4: Background extraction.** Let me check whether any setup repeats *identically* across scenarios and can be hoisted. Looking at the feature: every scenario runs `When I run drywall with the arguments "<args>"` — but `args` varies per scenario, so it can't move to Background. The `Given a source file...` steps vary by file/form/ids. There's no constant setup shared across all scenarios that preserves meaning if hoisted. A Background would not apply cleanly here — same conclusion as the prior feature, which has no Background.

No Background is warranted. Phase 4 complete.

# End-to-End QA Suite: JavaScript and TypeScript duplicate detection

Tracks: #5 (parent #1)
Feature file: `features/js_ts_target.feature`

## Scope

Verifies the JS/TS analysis targets entirely through the command-line
interface. QA spawns the compiled binary as a subprocess, passes fixture

# End-to-End QA Suite: JavaScript and TypeScript duplicate detection

Tracks: #5 (parent #1)
Feature file: `features/js_ts_target.feature`

## Scope

Verifies the JS/TS analysis targets entirely through the command-line
interface. QA spawns the compiled binary as a subprocess, passes fixture
directories and flags, and observes only exit code, stdout, and stderr. QA does
not call any project API and does not inspect internal structures (parser,
LangConfig, AST, hash sets, inverted index).

The user-interface affordances exercised are the positional `<path>` arguments,
the `--lang` flag with its new `js` and `ts` values, and the `--format` flag.
All other flags (`--threshold`, `--min-lines`, `--min-nodes`, `--exclude`)
behave as covered by the Rust duplicate-detection QA suite and are not
re-verified here except where a JS/TS fixture is needed to prove the same flag
works on the new targets.

This slice adds the JavaScript and TypeScript grammars to a pipeline whose
normalization, gating, scoring, sorting, and output formatting are already
covered by the Rust QA suite. QA here focuses on what is new: extension
auto-detection, the `--lang js`/`--lang ts` overrides, JS/TS function-form
recognition, and mixed-language (including cross-language) reporting.

## Preconditions

- The release binary is built (`cargo build --release`) at
  `target/release/drywall`.
- Fixtures are created on disk under a QA-controlled temp directory (e.g.
  `./tmp/qa-jsts/<case>/`); QA writes source files only, never touches project
  internals.

## Fixture builders (QA-side, on disk only)

- **TS-ARROW-TWIN**: two `.ts` files, each containing an arrow function bound to
  a `const`, with identical control flow and statement structure but different
  identifier and literal text; each clears the default gates (≥ 4 lines, ≥ 20
  nodes).
- **JS-DECL-TWIN**: two `.js` files, each containing a top-level
  `function` declaration, structurally identical modulo identifiers/literals,
  each clearing the gates.
- **TS-FORM/<form>**: two `.ts` files whose functions use a specific JS/TS
  function form — one of: function declaration, arrow function bound to
  `const`/`let`, class method, exported named function — structurally identical
  modulo identifiers, each clearing the gates.
- **EXT-PROBE/<ext>**: a single source file with a given extension (`.js`,
  `.jsx`, `.ts`, `.tsx`) containing one syntactically valid qualifying function
  for that grammar. Used to confirm the file is parsed by the expected grammar
  (observed as a clean, non-error run; no parse diagnostic on stderr).
- **FORCED-EXT**: a single file with a non-standard extension (e.g. `.txt`)
  whose contents are valid TypeScript (or valid JavaScript), used with `--lang`
  to confirm the forced grammar parses it.
- **MIXED-XLANG**: one `.rs` file and one `.ts` file whose functions are
  structural twins modulo identifiers/literals (the Rust function and the
  TS function normalize to overlapping subtree-hash sets), each clearing the
  gates.

## User-visible workflows

### QA-1 — TypeScript arrow-function twins reported as a pair (exit 1)
- Setup: TS-ARROW-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-jsts/ts-arrow/`.
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/ts-arrow`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: one `DUPLICATE` block naming both `.ts` fixture files with
  their line ranges; `score=` is ≥ `0.82`.
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-2 — JavaScript function-declaration twins reported as a pair (exit 1)
- Setup: JS-DECL-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-jsts/js-decl/`.
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/js-decl`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: one `DUPLICATE` block naming both `.js` fixture files; `score=`
  is ≥ `0.82`.
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-3 — `.ts` auto-detected as TypeScript without `--lang` (exit 1)
- Setup: TS-ARROW-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-jsts/auto-ts/` (extensions `.ts`).
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/auto-ts`  (no `--lang`)
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: the `.ts` pair is reported, confirming `.ts` files were parsed
  and analyzed by the TypeScript grammar without an explicit `--lang`.
- Expected stderr: empty (no parse diagnostic).

### QA-4 — `.js` auto-detected as JavaScript without `--lang` (exit 1)
- Setup: JS-DECL-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-jsts/auto-js/` (extensions `.js`).
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/auto-js`  (no `--lang`)
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: the `.js` pair is reported, confirming `.js` files were parsed
  and analyzed by the JavaScript grammar without an explicit `--lang`.
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-5 — `.jsx` and `.tsx` parse via the TypeScript grammar (exit 0, clean)
- Setup: EXT-PROBE/jsx and EXT-PROBE/tsx, each in its own directory
  (`./tmp/qa-jsts/ext-jsx/`, `./tmp/qa-jsts/ext-tsx/`), each holding a single
  valid file with no twin.
- Input A: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/ext-jsx`
- Input B: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/ext-tsx`
- Expected exit code (both): `0`
- Expected stdout (both): no `DUPLICATE` line (a single unpaired function).
- Expected stderr (both): empty — no parse-error text, confirming `.jsx` and
  `.tsx` were accepted by the TypeScript grammar rather than rejected.

### QA-6 — `--lang ts` forces TypeScript on a non-standard extension (exit 1)
- Setup: FORCED-EXT — two `.txt` files containing valid TypeScript twin
  functions in `./tmp/qa-jsts/forced-ts/`.
- Input: `drywall --lang ts ./tmp/qa-jsts/forced-ts`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: the pair is reported, confirming the forced TypeScript grammar
  overrode extension-based detection (which would otherwise skip `.txt`).
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-7 — `--lang js` forces JavaScript on a non-standard extension (exit 1)
- Setup: FORCED-EXT — two `.txt` files containing valid JavaScript twin
  functions in `./tmp/qa-jsts/forced-js/`.
- Input: `drywall --lang js ./tmp/qa-jsts/forced-js`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: the pair is reported.
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-8 — Each JS/TS function form is recognized as a candidate (exit 1)
- Setup: one directory per form under `./tmp/qa-jsts/form-<form>/`, each holding
  a TS-FORM/<form> twin, for forms:
  function declaration, arrow function (const/let), class method, exported named
  function.
- Input (per form): `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/form-<form>`
- Expected exit code (each): `1`
- Expected stdout (each): one `DUPLICATE` block reporting the twin pair,
  confirming the form was recognized as a qualifying analysis unit.
- Expected stderr (each): empty.

### QA-9 — Mixed-language directory analyzes each file with its own grammar (exit 1)
- Setup: MIXED-XLANG in `./tmp/qa-jsts/mixed/` — one `.rs` file and one `.ts`
  file that are structural twins.
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/mixed`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: a single `DUPLICATE` block naming the `.rs` file and the `.ts`
  file together — proving both grammars ran in one invocation and the
  cross-language pair appears in one merged, score-sorted output.
- Expected stderr: empty.
- Determinism: running the same input twice yields byte-identical stdout.

### QA-10 — `--format json` on a TS pair emits parseable JSON (exit 1)
- Setup: TS-ARROW-TWIN in `./tmp/qa-jsts/json/`.
- Input: `drywall --format json ./tmp/qa-jsts/json`
- Expected exit code: `1`
- Expected stdout: valid JSON containing exactly one pair object with `score`
  and `left`/`right` objects each carrying `file`, `start_line`, `end_line`, and
  `nodes`; the `file` fields carry the `.ts` paths.
- Expected stderr: empty.

### QA-11 — Unparsable JS/TS source is an error (exit 2)
- Setup: a `.ts` file containing syntactically invalid TypeScript in
  `./tmp/qa-jsts/unparsable/`.
- Input: `drywall ./tmp/qa-jsts/unparsable`
- Expected exit code: `2`
- Expected stderr: non-empty diagnostic.
- Expected stdout: no `DUPLICATE` line.
- Rationale: distinguishes a parse failure (exit 2) from a clean no-duplicate
  run (exit 0), now exercised on the TypeScript grammar.

## Observable states summary

| State | How QA observes it |
|---|---|
| Duplicates found | exit code `1` AND ≥ 1 `DUPLICATE` block (or ≥ 1 JSON pair object) |
| No duplicates | exit code `0` AND no `DUPLICATE` block (JSON: `[]`) |
| Error | exit code `2` AND non-empty stderr diagnostic |
| Grammar selected by extension | `.js` pair / `.ts` pair reported with no `--lang`; `.jsx`/`.tsx` parse without error |
| Grammar forced by `--lang` | `.txt` twin reported under `--lang ts` / `--lang js` |
| Cross-language pair | one `DUPLICATE` block naming a `.rs` file and a `.ts` file together |
| Deterministic output | identical input run twice yields byte-identical stdout |

## Out of scope for this suite

- Python analysis (deferred to a later slice).
- Re-verification of `--threshold`, `--min-lines`, `--min-nodes`, `--exclude`,
  sort order, and tie-break — covered language-agnostically by the Rust
  duplicate-detection QA suite.
- Internal verification of normalization, LangConfig node-type mapping, hashing,
  or Jaccard math (covered by the project's unit tests, not by this CLI-only QA
  suite).

# End-to-End QA Suite: JavaScript and TypeScript duplicate detection

Tracks: #5 (parent #1)
Feature file: `features/js_ts_target.feature`

## Scope

Verifies the JS/TS analysis targets entirely through the command-line
interface. QA spawns the compiled binary as a subprocess, passes fixture
directories and flags, and observes only exit code, stdout, and stderr. QA does
not call any project API and does not inspect internal structures (parser,
LangConfig, AST, hash sets, inverted index).

The user-interface affordances exercised are the positional `<path>` arguments,
the `--lang` flag with its new `js` and `ts` values, and the `--format` flag.
All other flags (`--threshold`, `--min-lines`, `--min-nodes`, `--exclude`)
behave as covered by the Rust duplicate-detection QA suite and are not
re-verified here except where a JS/TS fixture is needed to prove the same flag
works on the new targets.

This slice adds the JavaScript and TypeScript grammars to a pipeline whose
normalization, gating, scoring, sorting, and output formatting are already
covered by the Rust QA suite. QA here focuses on what is new: extension
auto-detection, the `--lang js`/`--lang ts` overrides, JS/TS function-form
recognition, and mixed-language reporting (each language's own pairs merged into

## Preconditions

- The release binary is built (`cargo build --release`) at

## Fixture builders (QA-side, on disk only)

- **TS-ARROW-TWIN**: two `.ts` files, each containing an arrow function bound to
  a `const`, with identical control flow and statement structure but different
  identifier and literal text; each clears the default gates (≥ 4 lines, ≥ 20
  nodes).
- **JS-DECL-TWIN**: two `.js` files, each containing a top-level
  `function` declaration, structurally identical modulo identifiers/literals,
  each clearing the gates.
- **TS-FORM/<form>**: two `.ts` files whose functions use a specific JS/TS
  function form — one of: function declaration, arrow function bound to

  modulo identifiers, each clearing the gates.
- **EXT-PROBE/<ext>**: a single source file with a given extension (`.js`,

- **FORCED-EXT**: a single file with a non-standard extension (e.g. `.txt`)

- **MIXED-LANG**: a directory holding a Rust same-language twin (two `.rs` files
  that are structural twins) and a TypeScript same-language twin (two `.ts` files
  that are structural twins), all four functions clearing the gates. The Rust and
  TypeScript structures differ from each other, so the only pairs that can form
  are the two within-language pairs.
